data.title: Precigen: The Best Potential Blockbuster You've Never Heard Of (Your ENT Has)
data.description:
Precigen Overview

My first "Buy" rating on Precigen (PGEN) was in August 2023 after its Recurrent Respiratory Papillomatosis (RRP) therapy was speeding to the market.

Following promising results (summarized above), Papzimeos was green-lit last August by the FDA.

When I last assessed PGEN in November (maintain "Buy"), management was already guiding for "cash flow breakeven" in 2026, which I thought implied over $150M in gross Papzimeos revenue.

While competition looms, I believed that Inovio's (INO) INO-3107 wouldn't pose much threat to Papzimeos due to the need for specialized in-office equipment (i.e., Inovio's CELLECTRA electroporation device).

Precigen's stock is higher today after revealing its 2026 outlook for Papzimeos.

Q1 Expectations

My expectations for Q1 revenue looked like this:

During the call, management stated that they "expect revenues in Q1 to exceed $18 million." This, effectively, falls very close to my "Bull" revenue band for Q1. Given that it is late March, this number is almost a "locked in" floor.

What's, perhaps, more encouraging are the 300+ patients in the hub, which is up 50% since January.

We also have to keep in mind that J-code does not kick in until April 1. The J-code will remove manual billing friction and likely will cause an efficiency "pop" in Q2.

The 65% commercial payer mix and "high teens to low 20s" gross-to-net margins suggest strong private insurance acceptance. That is, payers see value in Papzimeos and aren't pushing back too much on its orphan pricing.

Management is still guiding for cash flow breakeven in 2026, which appears more concrete with Papzimeos' Q1 revenue run rate already over $70M.

Pharma DCF

I have estimated before, based on "back-of-the-napkin" math, that Papzimeos could generate >$1B in peak annual sales. Now that I have more data (i.e., WAC, GTN), I want to formally model Papzimeos in Adult RRP.

In a recent investor deck, management estimates there are "approximately 27,000 adult patients in the US."

However, this is the top of the Target Population "funnel."

30% Peak Share reflects "standard of care" status.

A $368k Net Price is based on $460k WAC minus ~20% GTN.

I have WACC at 11% (higher to reflect single-asset dependency), COGS at 12%, Launch OpEx at 70% (for three years), and Steady-State OpEx at 30%.

To account for Inovio's INO-3107 likely market entry in 2027, I estimated a 20% Competitive Impact (impacting share assumptions).

So, I have Papzimeos at ~$800M in peak annual sales and worth $1.8B in net present value, or NPV.

Now, Precigen is currently in the middle of the regulatory review process in Europe. Approval is slated for late 2026 or early 2027. The company does intend to secure a commercial partner for the ROW.

What might this look like?

Of course, while Target Population is usually much higher in places like Europe than in the US, the commercial/payer/reimbursement dynamics are completely different.

As a rule of thumb, I typically assign a 0.3x to 0.5x ROW multiple.

PGEN's Intrinsic Equity Value estimate of $2.48B is derived from adding its pipeline NPV ($1.8B US + $720.8M ROW) with its cash and subtracting debt and overhead.

Because Precigen is projected for cash flow breakeven this year, there was no need to model for dilution. So, PGEN's Fair Value/Share of $7.01 is simply its Intrinsic Equity Value divided by its current Shares Outstanding.

In my mind, an ex-US partnership does two things. It provides upfront equity needed to advance the US launch, and it validates an asset's worth.

Based on my estimate for ~$721M ROW, here's what I expect a ROW partnership to look like:

Of course, I am not privy to any insider-type information. This is just my guess/what I'd like to see if my "Base" case materializes.

Scenario Analysis

Because I am not, admittedly, smarter than "the crowd," in most cases, I need to run a scenario analysis to "stress test" my optimistic (relative to what's implied by PGEN's current price) "Base" case.

My "Bear" case assumptions assume 10.5% Peak Share, which is pretty low considering that I had already adjusted the Target Population from 27k (the top of the "funnel") to roughly 8.3k. That said, 10.5% is not totally unrealistic. But it appears to assume a combination of challenging logistics and competition. I increased the WACC to 12.8%, which is quite high for an approved therapy. Finally, my "Bear" Net Price assumption nears $300k. But we know that GTN, to date, is favorable. PGEN is valued at $2.80/share in my "Bear" case.

My "Bull" case assumptions aren't too different from my "Base" case. Peak Share approaches 25% (this includes Competitive Impact assumptions) and WACC decreases to 10.4%. The Net Price doesn't have much room to go higher, so I placed it at 1.05x my "Base" assumption. In the "Bull" scenario, PGEN is worth $8.71/share.

Together (Bear/Base/Bull @ 35%/30%/35% weights), PGEN's probability-weighted value "falls" (relative to my "Base" case) to $6.13/share, which is still >50% higher than PGEN's current price (~$4.00/share).

Pipeline

My current model effectively assumes everything else in Precigen's pipeline is worth zero in risk-adjusted NPV. Although, in drug development, this is often an optimistic assumption because the vast majority of drugs fail in clinical trials.

But the company is pursuing a pediatric RRP clinical trial in Q4. Although not as large as a Target Pop. in adults, this would materially expand Papzimeos' market.

And as management slyly pointed out, its PRGN-2009 uses the same "AdenoVerse" platform as Papzimeos in HPV-associated cancers (e.g., cervical cancer and OPSCC).

Precigen's "UltraCAR-T" assets are in earlier-stage studies in hematological and solid tumors.

So although it's not entirely "complete," I'm satisfied with modeling just Precigen's opportunity in adult RRP barring major developments (i.e., pivotal data in pediatric RRP).

Conclusion

I remain positive on PGEN in 2026 (maintaining a "Buy" rating) as it starts to become apparent that Papzimeos has blockbuster potential. So I see the possibility of a stock re-rating this year that drives PGEN's price closer to my Fair Value/Share estimate of $7.01.

What are some risks?

It's totally possible that Precigen's Q1 numbers are the result of pent-up demand. If this is the case and Q2 and Q3 revenue does not accelerate, my 2026 estimate of $132M could be too aggressive. It could also preclude Precigen from breaking even this year, which may necessitate more funding (via dilution or more debt).

I may be underestimating the Inovio impact.

I may be overestimating Papzimeos' ROW value, which assumes successful reimbursement. European payers are less willing to pay six-figure orphan prices.

data.title: HP Pushes Work Experience Forward
data.description:
With the stunning rise of autonomous agents like OpenClaw and Anthropic’s (ANTHRO) Claude Work, as well as the staggering variety of opinions on what impact they will have on the future of work, it’s probably not terribly surprising to see renewed interest in the computers we all use at work. Toss in the fact that Intel (INTC) just released the commercial vPro versions of its new Core Ultra Series 3 (codenamed Panther Lake) processors, and well, you have the perfect storm to generate news on business PCs.

Or, in HP’s (HPQ) case, a nicely timed opportunity to debut a wide range of commercial PCs, notebooks, software and services and talk about the role they play in the Future of Work. And that’s exactly what the company did at their annual HP Imagine event, showing newly updated ProBook and EliteBook notebooks, ZBook mobile workstations, Z-series desktop workstations and more. They also shared survey information on the critical importance that having access to fully functioning PCs and printers have on our workplace productivity.

From a product perspective, a majority of the new commercial PCs that HP announced were based on Intel’s Core Ultra Series 3 processors. However, the company first talked about its latest EliteBook 6 G2q notebook based on Qualcomm’s (QCOM) newest Snapdragon X2 series chips and described it as the “world’s most configurable Arm-based PC.” This lightweight laptop is offered with either the 12-Core X2 Elite or the 60 or 10-Core X2 Plus SOC, and is available with numerous memory and storage options. In addition, there are options for HP Go, the company’s branded MVNO cellular data service, and a Qualcomm 5G modem. HP also showed off Intel and AMD-based versions of the same design, which is 15% thinner than previous generations. Other new PCs the company introduced include the EliteBook 8 G2 and the ProBook 4 G2, each with different physical designs, screen sizes, chip options and other configuration choices.

On the workstation side, HP debuted updated versions of its ZBook line, including the ZBook 8 G2, with options for both Intel and AMD CPUs, as well as the Intel Core Ultra Series 3-based ZBook X. As in the past, all of these units can be configured with various Nvidia (NVDA) discrete GPUs and, thanks to the greatly improved Xe3 graphics cores built into Intel’s latest SOC, you can also get a system with 12 Xe3 cores. On the desktop side, the newly redesigned Z8 Fury G6i offers the latest Intel CPUs, up to four Nvidia RTX Pro 6000 graphics cards and an interesting new future-proofing option. Specifically, HP showed off an optional expanded side cover (which also works with the Z4) that provides up to 15% more volume inside the case to fit future GPUs (which are expected to be too large to fit inside standard designs) as well as extra fans. Speaking of GPUs, the company also demonstrated a new version of its unique ZBoost GPU sharing software, which allows idle GPUs on nearby network-attached workstations to be called into service for certain tasks. The latest version now supports rendering workloads from applications such as Catia, TwinMotion and Siemens NX, as well as the AI-accelerated workloads from the first version of ZBoost.

Undoubtedly the most intriguing announcement that HP made was the debut of its HP IQ software application, which will be in early beta later this spring. Designed to be a type of local AI assistant, IQ offers an intelligent pull-down user interface (UI) from the top center of the screen where you can enter queries, and then sends those requests to an on-device model that can perform them for you, leveraging the data on your PC. In addition to the typical writing assistance, document summarization, and meeting recording and transcription capabilities that have become standard fare, IQ features several other interesting location-based features. Called HP NearSense and built around Google’s new D2DI (Device-to-Device Infrastructure) standard, these capabilities offer simplified connectivity and are being integrated into IQ.

The first of two NearSense features provides an easy way to find other local users and easily share files without having to connect, log in, or perform any of the other types of tasks normally involved with PC-based file sharing. Second, leveraging the connection with HP’s Poly conferencing room equipment, IQ-equipped PCs that are taken into meeting rooms featuring some of the latest-generation Poly Studio video bars will be automatically presented with the ability to connect to the assigned meeting. Admittedly, neither are earth-shattering on their own, but they are both solid examples of the kind of connected intelligence that modern PCs ought to have, so it’s great to see HP leveraging this new standard.

As a big believer in the potential of on-device AI, I love the concept of what HP is doing with IQ - and it’s great to see an application that will actually leverage the NPU of newer AI PCs as IQ does. However, I think it’s going to be challenging for enterprises and businesses to widely deploy this because of the many concerns that IT organizations have regarding any kind of autonomous agent. To be clear, IQ doesn’t have the kind of “access anything/do anything” capability of tools like OpenClaw, but it’s going to take a while for many larger organizations to adopt it. Plus, not surprisingly, it’s initially an HP-only application, which always creates challenges in multi-vendor PC environments (which many businesses have). An even bigger concern is that it requires the organization to be running HP’s WXP (Work Experience Platform), because that’s where organizations set up the guardrails and settings for the application. While I do believe WXP - which offers a variety of device management and telemetry tools for IT managers - has some powerful capabilities, it’s in an extremely crowded field, and many organizations already have other tools in place.

Despite my concerns, I give HP huge credit for making such a comprehensive effort with IQ, and I believe its initial UI offers an excellent way to integrate agentic AI applications onto a PC in an elegant, seamless manner. It’s definitely going to be interesting to watch, especially as more powerful models are able to start running locally on PCs.

The other important PC-related announcement that HP made at Imagine is on the security side. For years, HP has driven advanced security capabilities - though, unfortunately, they don’t get much credit for it. They continued that tradition at this year’s Imagine with the debut of TPM Guard, which is integrated into all the company’s latest PCs and workstations. The problem is that even BitLocker-encrypted data can now be hacked into via a low-cost hardware “snooping tool”. TPM Guard prevents those types of attacks by encrypting the physical communication between a PC’s CPU and its TPM (Trusted Platform Module) - a hardware security component found on all commercial PCs. It’s not something every user or every organization needs to worry about, but it is a critical hole that security-conscious environments have wanted to see filled. This, in connection with the new DTECT malware prevention technology that Intel has integrated into the commercial (vPro) versions of their Core Ultra Series 3 hardware, makes the latest PCs significantly better protected than previous generations. DTECT does real-time detection of malware leveraging AI models as well as the NPU and GPU built into the latest Intel commercial chips.

For a long time, interest around commercial PCs was frankly pretty limited. However, with the ability to run more powerful AI tools, perform on-device AI, and start thinking about autonomous agents, PCs have become very interesting once again. As HP demonstrated at Imagine, it is very much in the thick of these developments, and this year’s commercial PCs and workstations open up some very interesting opportunities.

Disclaimer: Some of the author's clients are vendors in the tech industry.

Disclosure: None.

Original Source: Author

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Hesai Group: Good Earnings Growth Outlook Ahead
data.description:
Hesai Group (HSAI) reported the company’s Q4 results on the 24th of March. The leading Chinese LiDAR sensor manufacturer again reported solid growth as the overall market continues to grow rapidly. Hesai’s domestic market position stands strong in China while the company plans to expand revenues with international OEMs. The growth outlook remains good going into 2026, looking to raise margins further through operating leverage.

I remained at a Hold rating in my previous August 2025 article on the stock, titled “Hesai Group: Surging Shipments Fuel Surging GAAP Profits.” The stock has since lost -20% of its value, meanwhile the S&P 500 (SP500) has returned 1%.

Hesai Q4 Review

Hesai’s Q4 was another solid quarter for the company. Total LiDAR shipments reached 631 thousand with fast 184% year-on-year growth, primarily driven by continued momentum in ADAS but also reflecting good momentum in robotics sales. The global LiDAR market has continued to grow very fast, and Hesai has managed to capture market growth very well as the company’s shipments continue to surge.

Although shipment growth was once again highly impressive, the Q4 topline performance missed Wall Street’s consensus quite clearly as fast growth is already expected. Hesai itself guided revenues of ¥1000-1200 million for the quarter, and total revenues ended up coming very near the lower bound at ¥1000.5 million. The average price per shipment has continued to go down sharply alongside a sales mix shift to mass-market ADAS, and revenues consequently grew at a much slower 39% rate.

Revenue growth remained fast but slowed down a bit from previous quarters; for the full year, Hesai reported 7 percentage points higher revenue growth of 46%. Ultimately, such a slowdown isn’t cause for much concern in my opinion, as quarterly revenue growth can fluctuate quite significantly.

Hesai’s underlying profitability continued to show major progress in Q4. Despite a declining revenues per shipment, economies of scale pushed Hesai’s gross margin up by 200 basis points to 41.0%. Revenue growth was much faster than the 13% increase in sales and marketing expenses, and -7% decline in G&A expenses, creating significant further operating leverage in earnings. Operating income came in at ¥102 million at a solid 10.2% margin. Excluding volatile other operating income that was extraordinarily high a year ago in Q4’2024, operating income improved by ¥158 million year-on-year to ¥42 million. I believe that Hesai should be sustainably profitable going forward, and revenue growth should continue to raise margins through operating leverage.

There should be fast growth ahead in 2026 as well as the LiDAR market continues to grow. Hesai guides 2026 LiDAR shipments at 3-3.5 million units, suggesting 101% growth from 2025 at the mid-point. The company is looking to increase its annual production capacity to 4 million units to support growing demand; the overall outlook remains good. Hesai's Q1 report will likely show slower growth as Hesai guides the quarter’s revenues at only ¥650-700 million, but revenue momentum should pick back up in following quarters as was noted in the Q4 earnings call.

Hesai’s domestic market position remains strong. The company has captured design wins with all top 10 Chinese automotive OEMs, cementing the company’s great domestic market position with more than 160 total vehicle models and increasing multi-LiDAR deals with leading OEMs. Hesai held a stable 33.8% market share of the Chinese LiDAR market during 2025. China’s regulatory environment is reaching an inflection point according to earnings call comments as Level 3 autonomy has been approved in cities such as Beijing and Chongqing, pushing OEMs to strive for autonomous systems with zero failure.

International markets are also developing well for Hesai; while China makes up a clear majority of Hesai’s current revenues, international markets represent clear future potential. Hesai’s partnership with Nvidia (NVDA) as the primary LiDAR partner positions Hesai the default option for OEMs for autonomous driving systems operating on the NVIDIA DRIVE Hyperion 10 platform. The Hyperion platform has significant partners, including Kia, Mercedes-Benz, Nissan, BYD, and other major automotive OEMs. Hesai could well start to capture more meaningful revenues from European, and North American, automotive OEMs in the future.

There's Some Upside in HSAI's Valuation, But Such Upside Comes With Uncertainty

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for Hesai. I assume quite similar revenue growth as previously at a 2025-2035 CAGR of 16.7%, driven by global LiDAR market growth. I again estimate the EBIT margin to ultimately stabilize at 20% as operating leverage should continue to improve margins.

The cash flow conversion should improve gradually as CapEx should moderate as a percentage of earnings. I expect Hesai to already generate positive free cash flow in 2026. I again assume an exit EV/EBIT of 20 after 2035.

The estimates put Hesai’s fair value at $25.9, suggesting 29% upside as of writing. The current stock price presents some base scenario upside, but for now, I believe that such upside mostly represents a fair margin of safety. The long-term revenue growth rate, and Hesai’s margin potential, are still uncertain as the market hasn’t matured yet.

The fair value estimate is down from $29.2 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 12.13%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I still don’t assume Hesai to hold a noteworthy amount of debt over the long term. For cost of equity, I use China’s 10-year bond yield of 1.81% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 5.14% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimate for China. I have kept the beta estimate at 1.93. With a liquidity premium of 0.4%, the cost of equity and WACC both stand at 12.13%.

Takeaway

Hesai’s growth story remains on track after the Q4 report. The LiDAR market continues to grow in size, and Hesai has a strong position in the important Chinese market while the company is eyeing international potential through a partnership with Nvidia. Profitability continues to improve alongside revenue growth.

HSAI stock has a fair amount of upside at the current price, but as long-term estimates still come with significant uncertainty, I remain at a Hold rating.

data.title: Grab Holdings: Local Network Effect Advantage And Dominant Market Position
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Virtus SGA International Growth Fund Q4 2025 Commentary.

Grab Holdings (GRAB) is a super-app for ride-hailing, food delivery, and digital payment services on mobile devices that operates in Singapore, Malaysia, Cambodia, Indonesia, Myanmar, the Philippines, Thailand, and Vietnam. The company was founded as MyTeksi by Anthony Tan and Tan Hooi Ling in 2012 to make taxi rides safer in Malaysia. By 2016, it was rebranded as Grab which coincided with the development of products for couriers. Grab was Southeast Asia's biggest technology startup and went public in 2021. Grab has a dominant market share (70%+) in ride hailing in most Southeast Asia markets except for Indonesia where market share is at 50%. It also owns a mapping service in the region, which enables more accurate routing and time estimates for drivers and better reliability and experiences for consumers. With a dominant position, Grab has a local network effect advantage which improves further as volume and density in its network increases. The majority of revenues are derived from repeatable transactions such as habitual food delivery and local travel. Recent conversations with management indicated that user growth and user frequency is accelerating, and that there is a stable competitive landscape as most countries are becoming duopolies with Grab taking the majority market share. There remains a significant addressable market in Southeast Asia, providing a lengthy growth runway, and Grab aims to continue leveraging its platform to grow use cases and consumer penetration, such as its low-cost products for both mobility and delivery, and premium products for mobility services.

Among the risks we are monitoring are a competitive landscape which can feature pricing promotions and a more price sensitive consumer, as well as the macroeconomic risk associated with operating across primarily emerging markets. Grab's single platform for multiple services helps to mitigate disruption from short term competitor promotions, and the company's dominant market position provides a healthy user base for growth of new businesses. Grab's diversification across eight countries helps to mitigate the risk of single currency and macroeconomic fluctuations. We will also be closely monitoring Grab's execution in newer business lines such as grocery and in-store that are at an early stage of development and banking which management has less experience with. Grab is leveraging its extensive user base and technological expertise to drive growth across new business lines. We initiated a below-average weight position in Grab in November and will build it opportunistically going forward.

Additional Disclosure

Risks:

Equity Securities: The market price of equity securities may be adversely affected by financial market, industry, or issuer-specific events. Focus on a particular style or on small, medium, or large-sized companies may enhance that risk.

Foreign & Emerging Markets: Investing in foreign securities, especially in emerging markets, subjects the portfolio to additional risks such as increased volatility, currency fluctuations, less liquidity, and political, regulatory, economic, and market risk.

Market Volatility: The value of the securities in the portfolio may go up or down in response to the prospects of individual companies and/or general economic conditions. Local, regional, or global events such as war or military conflict, terrorism, pandemic, or recession could impact the portfolio, including hampering the ability of the portfolio's manager(s) to invest its assets as intended.

Limited Number of Investments: Because the portfolio has a limited number of securities, it may be more susceptible to factors adversely affecting its securities than a portfolio with a greater number of securities.

Industry/Sector Concentration: A portfolio that focuses its investments in a particular industry or sector will be more sensitive to conditions that affect that industry or sector than a non-concentrated portfolio.

Currency Rate: Fluctuations in the exchange rates between the U.S. dollar and foreign currencies may negatively affect the value of the portfolio's shares.

Depositary Receipts: Investments in foreign companies through depositary receipts may expose the portfolio to the same risks as direct investments in securities of foreign issuers.

Geographic Concentration Risk: A portfolio that invests in a particular geographic location will be sensitive to financial, economic, political, and other events negatively affecting that location and may cause the value of the portfolio to decrease, perhaps significantly.

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Perfection Is A Fragile Thing To Hold: Why Sandisk Is A Sell (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Sandisk Corporation (SNDK) hasn’t necessarily gone out of the market’s favor, but is definitely losing steam ever since it hit a new all-time high of $777.6 just a few days ago. Today, the stock was down around 6.66% at open, but has since deepened its losses, down over 8% to $622.

The stock is now down over 12.5% over the past 5 days, but has still had explosive returns over the past 6 months, up around 570%, year to date, up 165%, and over the past year, up around 1100%.

I last covered the stock in late January, initiating it with a Buy into earnings. That has aged well, with the stock up 31% since, outperforming the market, as the S&P 500 (SP500) was down around 6% during the same period.

My thesis going into earnings was fairly simple: I saw near-term momentum as AI-driven storage demand and supply constraints fall right into Sandisk’s top line, as the company continues to benefit from tight NAND supply, surging memory prices, and hyperscaler SSD demand. At the risk of repeating myself, below was my take.

Still, I did say that this wasn’t a “buy and sleep well at night” kind of investment, “but rather a momentum-driven earnings play.” I think the stock giving out steam at current levels is exactly what I expected at some point. After a 1,100% run over the past year and a fresh all-time high just days ago, the stock was priced for perfection, and perfection is a fragile thing to hold.

Why the pullback:

The two headwinds hitting Sandisk aren't trivial. Google's (GOOG) TurboQuant compression algorithm is a direct shot at the "AI needs unlimited storage" narrative that has been the engine of this entire rally. Micron (MU) was also down around 4% on Wednesday, Seagate Technology (STX) down another 5.6% on the day, and Sandisk down another 6.5%. The sector wide selloff is continuing on Thursday.

While this may not change the fundamentals overnight, it introduces doubt into a story that had zero room for doubt at $777, and so is the case with peers. For a company like SanDisk, whose entire bull case rests on AI data centers consuming ever-increasing amounts of storage, this is an uncomfortable development. If AI models can be compressed to run more efficiently on less memory, the demand outlook for NAND flash storage, SanDisk's core product, becomes less certain. It doesn't break the thesis today, but it introduces a question mark into a story that was previously treated as a one-way bet, and that's enough to spook investors sitting on four-digit percentage gains.

According to Google’s blog post,

“TurboQuant proved it can quantize the key-value cache to just 3 bits without requiring training or fine-tuning and causing any compromise in model accuracy, all while achieving a faster runtime than the original LLMs (Gemma and Mistral). It is exceptionally efficient to implement and incurs negligible runtime overhead.”

The Nanya investment adds to the unease. Deploying $1 billion into a minority stake in a Taiwanese supplier is a strategic move that makes sense on paper, but the timing, the geopolitical exposure, and the lack of control it provides are all valid reasons for investors to raise an eyebrow.

At current levels, the risk-reward is no longer favorable, in my opinion, which is why I’m downgrading the stock to a Sell until further notice. This was a momentum trade riding a very specific macro wave: tight NAND supply, surging memory prices, and AI infrastructure capex running hot. All three of those tailwinds remain broadly intact, but the stock doesn't need bad news to roll over; it needs the news to stop being great.

For anyone who bought into the earnings play in late January, the trade has delivered. Taking some chips off the table here is respecting the fact that a 31% gain in under two months, on top of a 1,100% year, means the easy money has been made.

Investment thesis:

SanDisk's fiscal second quarter was a blowout by every measure.

Revenue came in at $3.03 billion, up 61% year-over-year and well ahead of the $2.69 billion consensus. EPS of $6.20 also came in nearly double the $3.62 consensus. That operational leverage translated into $980 million in free cash flow for the quarter, giving SanDisk meaningful capital to reinvest at exactly the moment when hyperscaler demand is accelerating. The datacenter segment, where the AI story is most direct, grew 76% year-over-year as hyperscalers rushed to lock in enterprise SSD capacity.

But the guidance is what I saw as most impressive here, with management expecting for Q3 revenue of $4.4 billion to $4.8 billion and EPS of $12 t0 $14, more than double the $5.11 analysts had expected at the time, which led the stock to surge over 20% in premarket trading on the print.

That being said, these expectations set up an enormous bar for the May earnings report. If SanDisk delivers on those numbers, the valuation starts to look rational, but if it misses, or if management's tone on AI demand softens even slightly in the wake of developments like TurboQuant, the stock with such gains has a long way to fall. I’m keeping an eye out for a more attractive entry.

Valuation and what’s next

While valuation is now more attractive than it was when I last covered the stock, my call was never on valuation.

On forward metrics, SanDisk looks reasonable relative to its storage peers. The forward EV/Sales of 6.37x sits both Seagate below at 8.38x and Western Digital (WDC) at 8.13x, with Micron the only name in the group cheaper at 3.91x. The forward EV/EBITDA of 12.72x tells a similar story, well below Seagate's 24.17x and Western Digital's 21.73x. But the number that really stands out is the forward PEG of 0.08, which is the lowest in the group by a wide margin.

On the technical side, the stock has pulled back sharply from its all-time high of $777 hit just days ago, now trading around $622. After a move of that magnitude over the past year, some consolidation is natural and arguably healthy, but the question is whether this is a normal breather or the beginning of something more sustained, particularly with the TurboQuant overhang now in the picture.

The next meaningful test is whether SanDisk can find its footing at current levels. If it doesn't, there's not a lot of technical support below to slow the slide in a stock that ran this fast, this far. The stock just broke the support at the EMA21 at $658, while the EMA50 at $580 remains the longer-term floor, along with the EMA200 at $324. RSI is also turning bearish at 47, so until momentum is back into the stock, I would recommend investors avoid this one.

data.title: Virtus SGA International Growth Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Investment Objective

SGA builds high-conviction portfolios focused on quality growth businesses that are anticipated to achieve consistent mid-teens earnings growth with reduced variability, supported by predictable revenue and cash flow generation. The firm's investment goal is to convert this stable portfolio earnings growth into a return profile designed to protect and reliably compound client wealth over time.

Performance

The fourth quarter of 2025 marked the end of a particularly challenging year for quality growth investment strategies across international markets. This period was defined by an extraordinary divergence in market returns. Cyclical and momentum-driven assets dominated, while quality growth strategies faced continued headwinds from sales stability underperformance.

Highlights

  • Q4 returns were 1.2% (Gross) and 0.4% (Net) compared to the MSCI ACWI ex USA (Net TR) return of 5.1% and the MSCI ACWI ex USA Growth (Net TR) return of 2.6%.
  • Since inception: Annualized returns of 7.3% (Gross) and 4.2% (Net) compared to 6.7% for MSCI ACWI ex USA (Net TR) return and 6.6% for the MSCI ACWI ex USA Growth (Net TR) return.
  • Market leadership in Q4 remained unfavorable for SGA's quality growth investment style as lower-quality and cyclicals led in an increasingly speculative market backdrop.
  • Largest Contributors for the quarter were Fast Retailing, TSMC, and Haleon.
  • Largest Detractors for the quarter were Alibaba, Arm Holdings, and Dassault Systemes.
  • Initiations – New holdings were introduced in Sea Limited and Grab Holdings.
  • Liquidations – Atlassian was sold in the quarter.
  • SGA sees continued opportunity in attractively priced, predictable, cash-generative and growing businesses, which should become more sought after if market volatility increases and growth decreases.

The International Growth portfolio has generated 7.3% (Gross) and 4.2% (Net) annualized returns since inception, compared to 6.7% and 6.6% for the MSCI ACWI ex USA (Net TR) and ACWI ex USA Growth (Net TR), respectively. Over the trailing one-year period, the SGA portfolio returned 10.6% (Gross) and 7.3% (Net) compared to 32.4% and 25.7% for the MSCI ACWI ex USA (Net TR) and ACWI ex USA Growth (Net TR), respectively. The International Growth portfolio generated 14% revenue growth and 11% earnings growth in 2025 and is forecast to achieve 10% revenue growth and 14% earnings growth, over the next three years, which is in line with historical averages.

Economic and Market Backdrop: The Cyclical Resurgence

The global economic landscape in 2025 defied earlier pessimistic forecasts. Growth expectations gradually increased, supported by resilient consumer spending and easing monetary policies implemented by the Federal Reserve, the Bank of England, and the European Central Bank. With all three major central banks in easing mode, global liquidity improved materially. This environment created powerful tailwinds for cyclical assets across international markets.

In Europe, the economic picture remained complex but leaned toward cyclical optimism in the fourth quarter. The ECB continued its rate-cutting cycle in response to falling inflation, which provided a boost to valuation multiples for interest-rate-sensitive sectors. However, the manufacturing core of Europe, particularly Germany, continued to face structural challenges related to energy costs and export demand. Despite these headwinds, European equity markets participated in the broad cyclical rally, with financials and industrials outperforming while higher-quality growth sectors such as software lagged. Political uncertainty in major economies like France further contributed to volatility, favoring short-term trading dynamics over long-term fundamental investing.

Japan continued to experience a unique macroeconomic environment characterized by the Bank of Japan's gradual policy normalization and fluctuating yen dynamics. Corporate governance reforms remained a bright spot, driving shareholder returns and encouraging efficiency. However, the market's preference shifted noticeably toward value and cyclical exposures, such as banks and trading houses, benefiting from higher yields and a weaker yen. Our high-quality growth holdings in Japan, such as Fast Retailing and Recruit, faced a mixed environment where operational excellence was occasionally overshadowed by macro-driven sector rotations.

Emerging Markets presented a divergent picture. In China, new stimulus measures introduced in late 2025 aimed at revitalizing the property sector and consumer confidence provided a short-term liquidity boost, sparking a sharp rally in lower-quality, high-beta names. However, structural concerns regarding long-term growth persist. Meanwhile, India continued to demonstrate robust economic fundamentals, though high valuations in certain segments led to consolidation. The portfolio's exposure to high-quality Indian franchises, such as HDFC Bank and Infosys, navigated this environment by focusing on execution and market share gains rather than relying on broad market beta.

2025 MSCI ACWI ex-USA Industry Returns

This environment favored cyclical industries indiscriminately. Banks, industrials, materials, and lower-quality balance sheet exposures rallied sharply on expectations of a soft economic landing, while high-quality companies with stable earnings growth received less attention. Despite this optimism, the narrative of uninterrupted acceleration carries risks. Inflation has not been fully subdued, and aggressive fiscal and monetary policies could reignite pricing pressures. Trade tensions and tariff uncertainty continue to threaten global supply chains. Current market pricing reflects an optimistic scenario with limited margin for error, particularly when contrasted with the valuation compression affecting high-quality growth holdings.

AI Capital Expenditures Update

A growing debate has emerged around the sustainability of the AI-driven capital expenditure boom. Critics have focused on accounting practices at hyperscale cloud providers and the potential for a "CapEx air pocket" if returns on investment do not materialize quickly. While much of this debate centers on U.S. mega caps, the implications are critical for international markets, particularly for semiconductor and software leaders.

SGA continues to believe the most attractive long-term AI opportunities reside not with hardware providers exposed to extreme cyclical swings, but with businesses that are building long-term value that possess proprietary data and integrated workflows. Our International portfolio is positioned to capture this value through companies that provide the essential intellectual property and manufacturing capability for the AI ecosystem.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. (TSMC)(TSM) remains the linchpin of the global AI supply chain. As the sole manufacturer capable of producing the advanced chips required for AI training and inference at scale, TSMC possesses a formidable competitive moat. Unlike commodity hardware providers, TSMC's pricing power and technological leadership insulate it from some of the commoditization risks facing the broader hardware sector. During the quarter, TSMC continued to benefit from high demand for advanced packaging and 3nm/5nm technologies.

Similarly, Arm Holdings (ARM) is crucial to the AI transition. As data centers seek greater power efficiency, Arm-based architecture is becoming the standard for next-generation CPUs. While the stock faced volatility in Q4 due to short-term guidance updates regarding R&D spending, the long-term thesis remains intact: Arm is well-positioned for double-digit earnings and revenue growth as royalty rates increase, and market penetration expands.

In the software space, European leaders like SAP (SAP) and Dassault Systemes (DASTY) are embedding AI into mission-critical enterprise workflows. SAP, for example, is leveraging AI to automate complex business processes within its ERP suite, driving cloud migration and higher average revenue per user. While the market has been slow to reward these "application layer" beneficiaries compared to the "infrastructure layer," we believe the latent potential in these holdings is significant. The current market focus on immediate hardware sales overlooks the enduring value of software companies that will monetize AI through high-margin, recurring revenue streams over the next decade.

We remain cautious regarding the sustainability of the current rate of global CapEx growth and see worrying signs of excess in the broader market. However, our holdings are selected for their ability to generate strong returns on capital and free cash flow regardless of the short-term CapEx cycle.

Largest Contributors

Fast Retailing

Fast Retailing (FRCOY) was a top contributor during the quarter, driven by strong execution across its global operations. China returned to growth in August and September, with management guiding for further improvement in both revenue and profit in the coming fiscal year, a turnaround following topline decline in China this fiscal year. International operations continued to show significant growth and margin expansion, particularly in North America and Europe, where the company continues to target good growth and further margin improvement. Fast Retailing's scale, efficient supply chain, and ability to offer quality products at attractive prices help navigate macroeconomic headwinds and currency volatility, while ongoing innovation in product design and marketing support brand strength and customer loyalty. With Japan accounting for one-third of Uniqlo's business and international regions poised for further expansion, Fast Retailing remains well positioned to capitalize on global growth opportunities across its diverse markets and deliver double-digit revenue growth in the years ahead. We maintained an above-average weight position during the quarter.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing

TSMC (TSM) was a top contributor during the quarter, driven by robust demand for advanced semiconductor manufacturing and improved gross margins as AI continues to grow strong and the non-AI segment showed signs of recovery. Management raised its revenue growth guidance to the mid-30% range, and given continued strength in demand, AI-related growth targets are expected to move above the current mid-40% level. TSMC's leadership in cutting-edge process nodes remains undisputed. The company raised its CapEx guidance to support future growth and is expected to increase its AI revenue growth target, reflecting confidence in the secular expansion of high-performance computing and AI workloads. Strategic steps to diversify manufacturing outside Taiwan, including new facilities in Japan, Germany, and the U.S., further strengthen TSMC's position. With its technology leadership, strong customer relationships, and disciplined execution, TSMC remains well-positioned to deliver strong double-digit revenue and earnings growth over the coming years. We added to the position and maintained an above-average weight during the quarter.

Haleon

Haleon (HLN) was a top contributor to performance this quarter as results came in line with expectations, with a healthier balance between volume/mix and price driving more stable revenue growth. Despite a still-challenging U.S. consumer backdrop, most categories delivered steady performance, market share gains continued, and retailer inventory reductions set up a cleaner inventory base heading into 2026. Management's reaffirmed full-year guidance, confidence in returning to 4-6% organic growth next year as destocking concludes, and a stronger-than-anticipated start to the recently announced productivity program all supported improved sentiment. Consumer demand for Haleon's products has proven to be relatively inelastic across most categories it participates in, supported by its category leadership and expert-endorsed brands, while its needs-based products drive highly recurring revenues. Haleon has a long runway for growth through innovation, increasing household penetration, e-commerce expansion, and opportunities across new geographies and adjacent categories. We maintained an average weight position during the quarter.

Largest Detractors

Alibaba

Alibaba (BABA) was a detractor during the quarter after the company reported mixed fiscal Q2 results. While cloud revenue growth accelerated and margins remained stable, the core commerce business struggled with slowing growth and significant profit pressure, particularly in the quick commerce segment where heavy investment and intense competition led to a sharp decline in profitability. Management's strategy to prioritize market share over unit economics in quick commerce will result in higher order volume and user engagement with an increasing share of gross merchandise value expected to drive better efficiency and unit economics improvement. The company's quick-commerce segment has rapidly approached market leadership in daily food delivery, leveraging its vast Taobao user base and ecosystem assets such as Alipay and Amap, while also driving engagement and growth across core commerce. Ongoing investments in AI and cloud infrastructure are expected to increase in coming quarters, and AI demand is broad with many traditional industries and enterprises ramping adoption while cloud penetration in China remains low. We also expect ongoing investments in quick commerce and food delivery services to sustain market share and user engagement, although at a lower intensity compared to the initial investment stage. We remain confident in Alibaba's ability to generate high-teens earnings growth over the next three years given its scale, innovation, and strong position in China's digital economy. We trimmed the position during the quarter, maintaining an above-average weight.

Arm Holdings

Arm Holdings (ARM) was a detractor during the quarter, despite a strong fiscal Q2 earnings report with revenue up 34% and profit 43%. Royalty revenue grew 21%, driven by triple digits data center growth and higher smartphone royalty from compute subsystem (CSS) customers. License revenue increased 56%, or 17% excluding related-party design service fees from SoftBank. The next quarter guidance for sales growth of 25% and profit up 20% was also ahead of expectations. However, the company faced several headwinds that weighed on investor sentiment. Elevated R&D spending, mostly related to Arm's design service for SoftBank, will impact Arm's margin outlook for next year. In addition, SoftBank is borrowing against its Arm investment to finance its investment into OpenAI, which raised concerns over forced selling of Arm shares if SoftBank does not secure sufficient funding for its investment commitments. Despite these near-term challenges, we remain confident in Arm's growth outlook, and we expect Arm to provide greater clarity over its chip project with SoftBank in 2026. We maintained a below-average weight position during the quarter, adding to the position on weakness.

Dassault Systemes

Dassault Systemes (DASTY) was a detractor during the quarter as the company reported a softer-than-expected third quarter, with sales up 5% excluding foreign exchange impact and earnings up 10%. The primary headwind was the accelerated transition to a SaaS model, which drove a decline in licensed revenue and, while recurring revenue outperformed, was not enough to offset the shortfall. Management's guidance for the fourth quarter was also reduced, reflecting both the ongoing SaaS transition and weaker trends at Medidata, where a slowdown in clinical trial starts and increased competition at the lower end of the market resulted in weaker growth than expected. Despite these challenges, subscription mix continues to expand and the company reiterated its expectation for subscription revenue to become the majority within three years, supporting a more predictable growth profile over time. Dassault Systemes remains a leader in enterprise software for industrial, healthcare, and consumer end markets, offering a portfolio of mission-critical solutions that command strong pricing power and high customer retention, while its transition to a recurring revenue model is expected to enhance long-term growth and margin resilience. Despite near-term headwinds, we believe Dassault's unique positioning in design, simulation, and life sciences software, combined with its expanding recurring revenue base, underpins a compelling outlook for sustained earnings growth and improved cash flow conversion over time. Considering the near-term challenges, we lowered the position target to a below-average weight.

Portfolio Activity

New positions were established in Sea Limited and Grab Holdings, while the portfolio's position in Atlassian (TEAM) was liquidated. Adyen (ADYEY), AIA Group (AAGIY), Arm Holdings, TSMC, and SAP (SAP) were added to on weakness, while Alibaba, FEMSA (KOF), Heineken (HEINY), Shopify (SHOP), Steris (STE), and Wal-Mart de Mexico (WMMVY) were trimmed.

Purchases

Sea Limited

During the quarter, we initiated a new position in Sea Limited (SE), a Southeast Asian consumer internet company with an integrated ecosystem combining e-commerce, digital payments, and entertainment. Sea has a diversified business model, with its Shopee e-commerce platform, a mobile-centric marketplace that provides integrated payments, logistics infrastructure, and seller services; Garena, a digital entertainment platform for users to access mobile and PC online games; and Monee digital financial services offering credit, mobile wallets, payment processing, banking, and insurtech services. Sea operates in markets with unique geopolitical, cultural, and economic complexities, and the company is ahead in localizing its products and content, making it difficult for competitors to disrupt at scale. Sea's e-commerce business benefits from low penetration rates and rising digital adoption, while its logistics network and localized approach create meaningful competitive advantages that are difficult for competitors to replicate. The company's digital financial services segment is scaling rapidly, leveraging accumulated user data and e-commerce use cases to drive engagement and profitability. In gaming, Sea's deep understanding of local audiences and self-developed franchises like Free Fire are favorable for emerging markets audiences where low-end smartphones dominate. While near-term margin volatility is expected as Sea invests in logistics, fulfillment, and new initiatives such as free shipping membership, management's focus on building long-term competitive advantages and expanding addressable markets positions the company well for sustainable growth.

Among the key risk considerations are increasing competition from TikTok and Lazada (backed by Alibaba) in Southeast Asia, MercadoLibre in Brazil, and Coupang in Taiwan. TikTok has been able to rapidly grow market share leveraging its user traffic on social media and the market share dynamic between Shopee, TikTok, and potential new entrants needs to be closely considered. We are also monitoring the unique political, macroeconomic, and currency risks across the multiple markets Sea operates in. Additionally, a potential slowdown in the U.S. and China could weaken the region's economic development and lead to depreciation in local currencies. We believe the company is positioned to benefit from low market penetration and an above average GDP growth rate across its geographies.

Profitability and positive free cash flow are growing strongly supported by the company's earlier investments, which we expect will continue to drive repeatable revenues from a large installed base. We plan to build the position opportunistically moving forward.

Grab Holdings (GRAB)

Grab Holdings is a super-app for ride-hailing, food delivery, and digital payment services on mobile devices that operates in Singapore, Malaysia, Cambodia, Indonesia, Myanmar, the Philippines, Thailand, and Vietnam. The company was founded as MyTeksi by Anthony Tan and Tan Hooi Ling in 2012 to make taxi rides safer in Malaysia. By 2016, it was rebranded as Grab which coincided with the development of products for couriers. Grab was Southeast Asia's biggest technology startup and went public in 2021. Grab has a dominant market share (70%+) in ride hailing in most Southeast Asia markets except for Indonesia where market share is at 50%. It also owns a mapping service in the region, which enables more accurate routing and time estimates for drivers and better reliability and experiences for consumers. With a dominant position, Grab has a local network effect advantage which improves further as volume and density in its network increases. The majority of revenues are derived from repeatable transactions such as habitual food delivery and local travel. Recent conversations with management indicated that user growth and user frequency is accelerating, and that there is a stable competitive landscape as most countries are becoming duopolies with Grab taking the majority market share. There remains a significant addressable market in Southeast Asia, providing a lengthy growth runway, and Grab aims to continue leveraging its platform to grow use cases and consumer penetration, such as its low-cost products for both mobility and delivery, and premium products for mobility services.

Among the risks we are monitoring are a competitive landscape which can feature pricing promotions and a more price sensitive consumer, as well as the macroeconomic risk associated with operating across primarily emerging markets. Grab's single platform for multiple services helps to mitigate disruption from short term competitor promotions, and the company's dominant market position provides a healthy user base for growth of new businesses. Grab's diversification across eight countries helps to mitigate the risk of single currency and macroeconomic fluctuations. We will also be closely monitoring Grab's execution in newer business lines such as grocery and in-store that are at an early stage of development and banking which management has less experience with. Grab is leveraging its extensive user base and technological expertise to drive growth across new businesses lines. We initiated a below-average weight position in Grab in November and will build it opportunistically going forward.

Sales

Atlassian

During the quarter, we exited our position in Atlassian due to forced attrition. While Atlassian delivered solid results, including strong cloud revenue growth and ongoing product innovation, we see increasing uncertainty around the future demand for developer seats and the evolving boundary between human and AI-driven tasks. The company's recent announcement of End of Life for its data center product by 2029 adds further complexity, as larger enterprise customers may be forced to make migration decisions that could impact retention. Although Atlassian remains a leader in agile development and collaboration tools and continues to invest in cloud and AI-native offerings, we question the predictability of the company's long-term value proposition as artificial intelligence continues to reshape software development. We will continue to monitor Atlassian's evolution, but for now, have reallocated capital to higher conviction opportunities in Sea Limited and Grab Holdings.

Outlook: Conviction Through Extremes

Entering 2026, the portfolio is well positioned. The sustained focus on cyclicality and momentum has driven the quality factor to historically depressed relative levels. The valuation premium for high-quality stocks has compressed to levels typically observed only during periods of crisis. Similarly, the valuation of the SGA International Growth portfolio relative to the MSCI ACWI ex-USA is currently lows not seen since 2017. The premium typically placed on reliability and predictability of growth has evaporated in recent years as a result of momentum-chasing amid the AI infrastructure boom.

Relative Valuations Between SGA and ACWI ex-USA At Most Attractive Levels Since 2017

While the past year has been tough for the portfolio, we are heartened by the resilient growth opportunities across our holdings and the now historically attractive relative valuation of the portfolio. We find it unlikely that the market will continue to ignore the high-quality companies we own and believe the setup moving forward is very attractive as the portfolio is well positioned to benefit from a broadening of market leadership and softening of the extreme momentum dynamics that have dominated markets over the past three years. Our philosophy, process, and approach remain steadfast. We will continue to invest in high-quality companies where we see attractively valued long-term durable growth – an approach that has delivered good returns with lower levels of risk over time.

Thank you for your patience and partnership – we wish you all the best for 2026.
