data.title: Brixmor Property: Solid Fundamentals Reflected In Valuation (Downgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Brixmor Property Group (BRX) have been a moderate performer over the past year, gaining about 10% alongside paying a dividend. The open-air shopping center owner is seeing revenue inflect as it fills vacant anchor tenant spaces, and shares are now reacting to that improvement. I last covered shares in October, reiterating Brixmor as a “Buy,” and that call has played out with the stock adding 10% since then while the market has lost 4%. At the time I was targeting a fair value of $29-$30, a level shares reached and have pulled back slightly from. With updated financials, a new macro environment, and shares near my target, now is a good time to revisit BRX.

As a reminder, Brixmor is an owner of open-air shopping centers. It operates across much of the country with significant exposure to high-income markets around New York City and California as well as faster-growth markets like Florida and Texas. About 80% of its portfolio is anchored by a grocery store, and I view this niche as one of the most durable within commercial real estate (“CRE”). Consumers clearly prefer open-air layouts to enclosed malls, while grocery stores have less e-commerce risk and bring a steady flow of traffic, which enables smaller shops in the centers to thrive.

The company has a diverse and financially sound tenant base. Its top 10 tenants represent just over 20% of the business, and none face concerning financial stress. In fact, management expects just 75-100bp of lost revenue from tenant bankruptcies, a bit better than its prior 75-110bp long-term assumption, reflecting an improving tenant mix. While higher energy prices may weigh on consumer spending to an extent, I view BRX as largely removed from this pressure given the soundness of its tenant base.

Importantly, the company is reporting strong results, maintaining my expectations. In the company’s fourth quarter, reported on February 9, Brixmor generated $0.58 of funds from operations (“FFO”), which beat estimates by a penny as revenue surged 8% to $354 million. For the full year, the company generated $2.25, in line with my $2.24-$2.25 target. Given the recurring nature of its revenue base, near-term cash flows are highly predictable, so this is a company that should deliver results close to modeled assumptions.

In Q4, same-property net operating income (“NOI”) grew by 6%, thanks to 360bbps of base rent growth thanks to newly commenced leases. Revenues grew by 4.8% while expenses were up just 1.6%, enabling 80bps of margin expansion to 73.9%. Its billed occupancy was up 10bps to 91.7% as it makes progress occupying anchor spaces that had been under redevelopment.

Small shop occupancy ended the year at a strong 92.2%. Small shops tend to turn over more frequently than grocery anchors, resulting in structurally lower occupancy over the long term. However, at ~$30/square foot, these shops pay ~50% more rent than the company’s average, making them particularly lucrative. Essentially, anchors pay less, given their stability and the foot traffic that they bring to the center, with smaller shops paying more to offset their higher turnover.

New leasing has been strong, and in 2025, new leases were at a 39% premium to existing leases. I expect this new leasing spread to continue. Anchors with leases expiring over the next three years have rents that could rise about 70% to reach BRX’s current average on new leases. I do not expect this entire gap to be closed when leases are renewed, but there is clearly substantial room for growth. Plus, the operating cost of space is the same irrespective of rent, so this incremental rent should flow right to the bottom line.

The company has a $62 million pipeline of leases that have not yet commenced. It should see about $43 million of this commence in 2026, with the remainder in 2027. When a tenant signs a lease, it does not begin paying rent until the space is ready to be occupied. This provides a clear line of sight to generating incremental revenue. Many of these locations are anchor locations (from previously bankrupt tenants like Bed Bath & Beyond and Big Lots), and occupying them should have a positive halo effect across the entire center.

Importantly, Brixmor maintains a solid balance sheet with 5.4x debt/EBITDA leverage. Its maturities are well-laddered, and with $362 million of cash on its balance sheet, it has partially prefunded this year’s maturity. With debt having just a 4.3% weighted interest rate, refinancing maturities in the current rate environment will be a net headwind, and I expect interest expense to be a headwind for growth through 2028, a feature that is fairly common across the REIT sector.

Shares offer a 4.3% dividend yield. Brixmor has excellent ~1.9x dividend coverage, giving it one of the most secure dividends in the industry. As such, I expect continued dividend growth at a mid-to-upper-single-digit pace over the next few years. With its dividend coverage, BRX retains about $340 million of cash. That provides more than sufficient cash for redevelopment activity and bolt-on acquisitions. During 2025, it completed $420 million of acquisitions while disposing of $296 million in properties. This was the most gross acquisitions in history and the second biggest net purchase level. I expect BRX to remain active on the M&A front in 2026.

Looking forward, the company is targeting $2.33-$2.37 of FFO, assuming 4.5-5.5% same-property NOI growth. At the midpoint, this represents 4.4% growth, with strong operating results being partially offset by a ~1.5% headwind from higher interest expense as it has refinanced maturing bonds at higher yields. Management expected at least 4.5% of base rent growth in 2026.

Back in October, I was targeting $2.33-$2.38 of 2026 FFO, so Brixmor’s guidance is entirely consistent with my view. I continue to expect FFO to fall within this range. Shares are currently trading at an ~8% FFO yield, which is not overly expensive even in this elevated rate environment. Over the medium term, I believe this business can comfortably run at a ~1.6x dividend coverage ratio and expect dividend growth to outpace FFO growth until this is achieved.

That would enable a ~$0.37 quarterly dividend, about 20% above current levels. I expect this gap between the actual and the potential dividend will steadily close over the next few years. Given 3%-4% long-term growth, I view a ~4.5-5% potential dividend yield as appropriate, implying fair value in the $29-31 area. With shares approaching that level, I am downgrading Brixmor to a “hold.”

Given its solid business and leasing momentum, shares are suitable for income-oriented investors. However, I do not see the capital appreciation upside to make shares a “buy” after their recent run. On any pullback towards $27, I would add, but at current levels, shares are more of an income than a capital appreciation opportunity.

data.title: The Wealth Distribution
data.description:
I have been writing more and more about the fact that the growth of wealth inequality over the past fifty years or so has been a major change in the makeup of the American economy, and one must understand what changes have occurred to understand much of U.S. history over this time period.

Well, Rachel Louise Ensign has produced an article that captures much of the data change that people need to see and understand.

Ms. Ensign has produced this article for the Wall Street Journal--"They're Rich but Not Famous--and They're Suddenly Everywhere."

"The number of Americans worth tens of millions and hundreds of millions of dollars has boomed in the past few decades, thanks to a rising stock market, lucrative private investments, and swelling valuations for small and midsize businesses. This growing class is now a huge force in the economy, driving the demand for everything from lavish hotel rooms to private jet travel."

Here is a picture of what we are talking about.

Ms. Ensign writes:

"Over the past few decades, the growth in the number of very rich households has surpassed general population growth."

"Many well-off households have further benefitted from the big stock market gains of the past few years."

I won't take much more from the Ensign article because it would just be repeating what has already been written.

What I would like to emphasize, however, is the emphasis put on the "big stock market gains."

The U.S. economy over the past fifty years or so has performed very, very well, even though we experienced "the Great Recession" in the 2007-2009 period.

One of the major reasons for this good performance has been the shift in the way economic policy has been devised and how it has been conducted.

I go back to the writing of the economist Ben Bernanke from Yale University.

Bernanke's economic research, to me, is the foundation for what has happened since the 1980s.

Bernanke's ideas for economic policy ran something like this.

The Federal Reserve needed to make sure that stock market prices rose.

Rising stock prices raised consumer wealth, and rising consumer wealth contributed to rising consumer spending.

Rising consumer spending contributed to rising economic growth, which benefited the whole economy.

The first real application of this approach to macroeconomic policy came in the late 1970s and 1980s as Paul Volcker became the chairman of the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System.

Volcker "broke the back" of the inflationary dilemma faced by the U.S. in the 1970s and got the United States economy humming once again by getting the U.S. stock market going.

When Volcker left the Fed in 1987, the "new" approach to monetary policy was the foundation for what was to follow.

Alan Greenspan became the chairman following Paul Volcker, and Greenspan built on what Volcker had started, keeping up a "quantity" approach to monetary policy "front and center."

This was followed up by Ben Bernanke himself, as he became Fed Chair in 2006. Bernanke, during the Great Recession, took the Fed's monetary policy into the next phase...a period of quantitative easing or quantitative tightening.

The Fed has operated in this fashion ever since.

Jerome Powell, the current Fed Chair, has stuck by this approach, leading the Fed through almost four years of quantitative tightening...2022 through 2025...and then moving the Fed back into a position of quantitative easing from December 2025 to the present time.

My analysis assumes that since the late 1970s, the Federal Reserve has followed some kind of "quantitative" approach to monetary policy, coming from the research of Ben Bernanke, that aimed to, in one way or another, ultimately impact stock prices.

And here is how one major stock index performed during that period. Other indices followed similar paths.

Note that even during the Great Recession, the stock market performance did not waver that much.

Furthermore, the "big swing" in the 2020s was not a result of monetary policy at all. The "big swing" here resulted from the spread of the "COVID-19" pandemic. Also, the "big swing" was fought by Fed Chairman Jerome Powell, who saw to it that the Fed did everything it could to fight the impact of the pandemic by pumping reserves into the banking system.

The basic conclusion that one can draw from these data is that the wealth of the United States grew and grew and grew during this period of time, basically because the stock market was steadily growing.

And this growth in wealth got translated into a growing U.S. economy.

Bottom line...for the past 50 years or so, the United States economy has performed very well.

Since July 2009, the United States economy has grown at an annual compound rate of growth of 2.4 percent and experienced one recession, the shortest recession in U.S. history...two months.

Unemployment has been around a historical low of 4.0 percent or lower in recent years.

All in all, the performance has been quite good.

But there has been an undercurrent of discontent that has been growing in recent years.

Some experts argue that this discontent has resulted in some of the election results of recent elections. This undercurrent of discontent has gone against those that have been overly pleased with the general picture of economic/wealth expansion that I have drawn above.

The problem seems to be that even though substantial wealth creation has taken place over these last fifty years, the lower sixty percent of the wealth distribution in the country have not participated in the rise.

The data reported by Ms. Ensign gives us a very stark picture of what has happened to the wealth/income distribution in the country.

The bottom line is that not all have participated in the wealth-building of the past 50 years, and this fact is coming, more and more, to the surface as the country moves forward.

This fact may play more and more of a part in how the United States goes forward. It does not seem to want to go away!

data.title: Sarepta Therapeutics: It's Too Early To Get Excited About siRNA Data
data.description:
Investment Overview

Sarepta Therapeutics (SRPT) stock made a 35% gain yesterday, rising to a value of $34 per share (market cap of $2.5bn), after the company shared the first clinical results from two of its siRNA programs for neuromuscular diseases, SRP-001, indicated for facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy type 1 ("FSHD1"), and SRP-1003, indicated for myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1). In early trading today, shares are down ~7%.

Sarepta's short, interfering RNA ("siRNA") programs were developed using Arrowhead Pharmaceuticals (ARWR) targeted RNAi molecule ("TrIM") platform which is, according to Sarepta's 2025 annual report / 10K filing:

designed to deliver siRNA to multiple tissue and cell types throughout the body with the goal of initiating the RNA interference mechanism and inducing rapid and durable knockdown of target genes.

An overview of the platform is also provided in the slide below, taken from Sarepta's results presentation, shared yesterday:

Overview Of SRP-1001 Results

In a press release also shared yesterday Sarepta notes that FSHD is a rare genetic disease that causes progressive weakness of skeletal muscles:

caused by abnormal activation of the DUX4 gene on chromosome 4, leading to production of the DUX4 protein, which is toxic to muscle cells and drives muscle degeneration.

There are no cures or disease modifying therapies for the condition approved today, the company also notes. SRP-1001 is designed to reduce or "knock down" the production of DUX4 protein in patients' skeletal muscle.

Sarepta's Phase 1/2 randomised study involved single and multiple ascending dose ("SAD / MAD") cohorts, and enrolled 56 patients in total, 40 treated with varying doses of SRP-001 (up to a maximum 8.17mg/kg dose), and 10 treated with placebo.

Among the highlights from the study shared yesterday, Sarepta noted that:

  • a single dose of SRP-1001 delivers 6-fold greater siRNA muscle concentration than multiple doses of transferrin-targeting ligand and represents the highest siRNA dose with muscle PK data to date;
  • a single dose of SRP-1001 is providing suppression equal to or in excess of 90% across all 3 panels when adjusted to placebo - Sarepta says this represents the greatest DUX4 gene reduction in the field observed to date;
  • SRP-1001 drives rapid reduction of serum creatine kinase ("CK") with a single dose.
  • The safety profile is described as "reassuring", with no serious related treatment emergent adverse events ("TEAEs" reported). 77% of patients experienced TEAEs, and one patient experienced a serious TEAE deemed not to be study related.

Overview Of SRP-1003 Results

DM1 is a common form of adult-onset muscular dystrophy caused by repeat expansion in DPMK gene, Sarepta says, that affects skeletal and smooth muscle, and potentially the heart, respiratory system, eye, and endocrine, gastrointestinal and central nervous systems. It is a ~40k patient population in the US, with no cures or disease modifying therapies currently available.

SRP-1003 is designed to target or "knock-down" the DMPK messenger-RNA in target cells, halting the production of the disease causing protein.

The Phase 1/2 study was again a SAD/MAD study involving a placebo arm, with 36 patients treated with SRP-1003 across four cohorts, up to a maximum dose of 4.1mg/kg, and 11 patients receiving placebo.

The main highlights Sarepta shared yesterday included a dose dependent increase in plasma exposure, superior muscle delivery even with single low dose, and a "reassuring safety profile, although three serious AEs were reported in two patients - fatal arrhythmia, limb abscesses, and emotional distress, deemed unrelated to the study drug. 77.8% of patients experienced a TEAE.

Analysis - Sarepta Looking To Bounce Back After A Tough 2025

At the beginning of 2025, Sarepta was looking forward with confidence - as I explained in a note for Seeking Alpha shared in January last year, the company was projecting for ~$3bn of revenues in 2025, led by its gene therapy for Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy ("DMD") therapy Elevidys, which management believed would experience the most successful commercial launch of any gene therapy ever.

That wasn't to be the case however. Sarepta stock crashed in value and as I wrote in note on the company in October last year (assigning a Hold rating, following a Sell rating I assigned in July last year):

In Q1 2025, Sarepta reported Elevidys revenues of $375m, and total revenues of $612m, but the company had disclosed in March that a patient receiving treatment with Elevidys had died from acute liver failure ("ALF"), and in June, it reported that a second death had occurred, also from ALF. In July, Sarepta reported the death of a third patient, this time of a non-ambulatory limb-girdle muscular dystrophy ("LGMD") patient participating in a Phase 1/2 trial for Sarepta's gene therapy candidate SRP-9004.

Sarepta suspended all commercial shipments of ELEVIDYS to non-ambulatory patients and voluntarily stopped shipments of the drug to ambulatory patients, although it has since resumed the latter, in agreement with the Food and Drug Administration ("FDA").

As per Sarepta's 2025 annual report / 10K filing:

The Company recently announced the conclusion of the label supplement for ELEVIDYS, including the addition of a boxed warning for risk of ALI and ALF and the removal of the non-ambulatory population from the Indication and Usage section.

Elevidys - marketed and sold with Pharma giant Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY) - in fact failed to meet the primary efficacy endpoint in its pivotal EMBARK study, although it did demonstrate clinically meaningful and statistically significant benefits in both key secondary functional endpoints: time to rise from floor and 10 minute walk/run test, which was enough to secure a full approval from the FDA for ambulatory patients.

Also last year, Sarepta reported that two of its EXON skipping drugs developed to treat DMD, Vyondys 53 and Amondys 45, which had been granted an accelerated approval (approval based on a biomarker indicative of a clinical benefit, but not itself a measure of clinical benefit) in 2019 and 2021, had failed to meet the primary endpoint of a post-confirmatory study.

In the end, Sarepta announced full-year 2025 revenues of $1.86bn, with Elevidys contributing $898.7m of revenues, and its exon-skipping products $965.6m of revenues. Total revenues were reported as $2.2bn, with a GAAP net loss of $(713m), of $(7.13) per share. Last July, the company announced a corporate restructuring in July last year, which may see up to 36% of the workforce depart. In February this year, Sarepta's CEO Doug Ingram also announced his retirement from the company.

Sarepta also reported ~$1bn in cash as of the end of 2025, and says it anticipates remaining profitable and cash‑flow positive in 2026. The company is projecting $1.2bn - $1.4bn of revenues for Elevidys in 2026.

As we can see above, there are many data readouts and business updates planned in the next 12-18 months. Elevidys will read out more data, Sarepta will update on the outcome of an FDA meeting to discuss the confirmatory Vyondys / Amondys study data and a Huntington's Disease therapy will enter clinical studies. As CEO Ingram discussed on yesterday's call:

To remind you, we have 5 programs in clinical development right now. We have FSHD and DM1. We have IPF, SCA2 and Huntington's disease. We have 2 other programs in animal testing, and we have 6 research programs that make up this portfolio of siRNA. Our 5 clinical programs alone offer potential therapy to as many as 160,000 patients in the United States and multiples greater than that worldwide.

Analysis - Will Yesterday's Data Spark A Long Term Rise In Sarepta Stock?

I think there are essentially two ways of looking at Sarepta's business today - a bullish and a bearish. Let's start with the bearish.

Ultimately, Sarepta may have four drug products on the market, all indicated for DMD, but three of these have failed to meet key primary endpoints in key studies, and there is a risk that the FDA could ask the company to take Vyondys and Exondys off the market this year. Fears around prescribing Elevidys due to safety concerns could also undermine that products' sales going forward.

Meanwhile, there are many current and future competitors in the DMD space. Japanses Pharma Nippon Shinyaku has an exon 53 skipping therapy, Viltepso on the market, and is developing two products to treat patients living with Duchenne that have mutations amenable to exons 44 and 50, respectively.

Italfarmaco also has an approved DMD product, Givinostat, and the list of pharmas and biotechs developing DMD therapies includes Wave Life Sciences (WVE), Dyne Therapeutics (DYN), Avidity Biosciences (RNA), Entrada Therapeutics (TRDA), Regenxbio (RGNX), Biomarin (BMRN), Solid Biosciences (SLDB), Insmed (INSD), and Capricor Therapeutics (CAPR).

Sarepta made a loss of <$(700m) last year, and has only ever been profitable in 2024, having typically lost >$(500m) per annum in prior years as far back as 2018. In summary, the bear case is built on products with limited treatment effect, heavy losses, intense competition, safety issues, lack of forward guidance / falling revenues, and the departure of a long-serving CEO.

If that paints the picture of a company in crisis, let's consider the bull case.

This is built on the fact that while Sarepta's therapies may not have met certain endpoints, they did meet others, and are arguably the best available therapies that have treated patients with few or no other viable treatment options available for many years, resulting in - anecdotally at least - significant improvements in many patients.

Elevidys remains on the market, with "blockbuster" revenues forecast for 2026, and the company is advancing new therapies to market, which have demonstrated some intriguing, albeit early stage data. We might therefore speculate that a market cap of $2.3bn for a company promising revenues in the billions, profitability and to be cash flow positive in 2026 is too low.

How does the data shared yesterday change the picture? For me, the issue that dogs Sarepta - and most other companies developing therapies for muscle wasting diseases - is the inability to generate truly statistically significant efficacy in patients, instead relying on biomarker data suggestive of benefit to secure approvals for drugs that command - in Elevidys' case - multi-million dollar list prices.

I consider yesterday's data to be more of the same - yes, there are signs that the therapy is reaching the target cells, and generating some promising biomarker data, but will that ultimately result in the drug's ability to meaningfully improve patients conditions - I think that is hard to state with any real conviction, as former encouraging biomarker data has arguably not translated to positive treatment outcomes.

It's a difficult subject - some benefit is arguably better than no benefit, but when we consider TEAEs, serious TEAS, and costs, and the inability to meet a primary endpoint in a pivotal study - is it even possible to make that argument?

As such, I would caution against reading too much into yesterday's data and while I wish Sarepta the best of luck with its studies, I'd be firmly on the side-lines as regards the investment opportunity here. There have been too many false dawns, and late stage efficacy data is what truly matters.

data.title: Nike At 5-Year Lows: Why The Turnaround Is Already Working
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Investment Call: Strong Buy

The Nike, Inc. (NYSE: NKE) de-rating was among the worst in the history of the large-cap consumer brands. In early 2021, it hit an all-time high of about $179 but in March of 2026, it was down over 70% to about $53 per share, burdened by a tariff headwind worth about $1.5 billion per year and the price of a complete operational reset. This is the first time Nike has not traded at these levels since 2017. Nike has unmatched brand awareness, despite these headwinds, ranked #1 in unaided consumer awareness in athletic footwear and apparel worldwide. The offer by Elliott Hill to restructure the company in a systematic way is showing a positive curve in various operational indicators. Nike is not a structurally handicapped organization but a well-capitalized franchise with a planned re-set to sustainable long-run profitability. The 12-month price forecast of $85 is a pointer to mid-cycle EBIT revival and low multiple re-rating as the "Win Now" strategy gains traction.

The investment thesis is based on five components that are interconnected. Marketing spend is projected to exceed $5 billion in FY2026 according to LSEG estimates, and Nike has no fewer athletic partnerships than Michael Jordan, LeBron James, Cristiano Ronaldo, Serena Williams and hundreds more in every major sport. Although On, Hoka, and New Balance have achieved considerable gains in some segments, it would be a major challenge for any new or current rival to replicate over 50 years of brand heritage and global association with athletes. The new CEO Elliott Hill has 32 years of experience in business development and retail relationships at Nike before retirement in 2020. The first thing Hill has done to fill the leadership void left by John Donahoe is to recover wholesale as the foundation of the distribution economics of Nike and salvage the relationships that were destroyed. The channel also showed evident progress: according to the Q2 FY2026 release, wholesale revenues increased by 8% in comparison with the previous year to $7.5 billion, and North American wholesale increased +24%. Nike's running division—its most strategically important sport segment—has maintained growth of more than 20% in two quarters straight, which is real brand momentum as opposed to in-promotion response. NKE shares are currently priced at around 22x FY2027E consensus EPS of $2.43 versus the market which is remaining cautious to the point of future Q3 FY2026 earnings release at its historical mid-cycle multiple of about 22x.

Q2 FY2026 Results: The Beat the Market Couldn't Celebrate

The Nike FY2026 fiscal second quarter expectations, released on December 18, 2025, showed a strong difference between investor perception and business trajectory. Its diluted Nike EPS is also priced at $0.53, which is roughly 43 percent above the estimated price of $0.37. It is the first time that the full year revenue of the company is compared positively after many years of decline to the single digit level of $12.4 billion. The 2nd consecutive quarter revealed a growth of North American revenue by 9% and a growth of the global wholesale revenue by 8%.The running business increased by over 20%. The stock declined about 11% in the following days after the announcement as the investors paid attention to the gross margin shrinkage of 300 basis points to 40.6, a result of the annual tariffs and inventory obsolete costs (1.5 billion). The management showed caution about low-single-digit revenue depreciations in Q3.

The shrinkage of the margin can be attributed to two transient variables. The management is also striving to mix the shift of the fiscal year 2026 and the pricing endeavors to counteract tariff headwinds. The gross tariff headwind in FY2026 would be about 315 basis points to gross margin, as was mentioned in the Q2 earnings call, and it is projected that the net effect of mitigation measures will reduce it to about 120 basis points. Hill has reduced channel legacy styles that were operating on below-average margins. The point in inventory liquidation charges by Hill is to release available shelf space and give the consumer a sampler of the new products they are offering at their retail price. In the earnings call, CFO Matt Friend pointed to the fact that the lack of tariffs would have seen costs increase, and powertrain operations enjoyed the result of long lead times and sales to push down prices.

There is also high strategic flexibility in the Nike balance sheet. As of November 30, 2025, they had liquid assets of $8.35 billion in the form of $6.97 billion and $1.37 billion of cash and short-term investments, respectively, and a market value of approximately $82 billion. As the Nike IR announcement shows, the current ratio of 2.1 reveals that there is no short-term liquidity pressure and no pressure to refinance in the near future. In November 2025, the board announced a quarterly dividend of $0.41, or 3% higher than the previous dividend of $0.40 ($1.64 per annum), and this is the 24th consecutive annual dividend increase. The free cash flow is the only way the company can attain the dividend of $1.64 billion. Besides the shares that were bought by the CEO, Elliott Hill, the data assembled by the board chairman of Nike, Tim Cook, shows that the most knowledgeable individuals regarding the future of the company are making the most of their own work.

The "Win Now" Strategy: Five Pillars, Measurable Progress

The five pillars of the "Win Now" program, i.e. culture, product innovation, marketing investment, marketplace rebalancing, and in-person experience, also referred to in FY2025 Q3 of the earnings call by Elliott Hill. The strategy represents a premeditated course of correction of the course followed by the previous management, literally overturning a number of decisions made in 2020–2024. The business strategy is reformulated at Nike: the business strategy switched into de-emphasizing its wholesale partners, replacing brand marketing with performance marketing, and Nike was forced off Amazon to reduce its costs and the number of wholesale partners. The promotion was detracting from the brand equity and favoritism to the competition, such as Hoka and New Balance, with regard to incremental floor space and attention, which is at the cost of brand equity. The departure of Nike from Amazon predetermines the withdrawal from the most popular online discovery and purchase platform in the US.

But Hill has already changed their minds, and there has been a resounding victory. Nike returning to the Amazon marketplace has revitalized the attention of the approximately 38% of American consumers that mainly use the site to search and buy sporting footwear. In September 2025, Dick's Sporting Goods acquired Foot Locker, creating a massive wholesale partner that served about $5 billion of the annual Nike profits, and thus the wholesale strategy became easier due to the inclusion of bigger and more committed wholesalers. Nike has rebuilt the relationship with its key wholesale clients through a new model of partnership, and the company is more involved in the product planning, marketing investments, category assortment discipline, and high-level in-store display. The initial and most impressive data that a re-engagement strategy is yielding results is the North American wholesale growth rate of 24% in the second quarter of fiscal year 2026.

The innovation engine of Nike has been restored, as shown in the 20%+ growth in the running category of the company in two quarters. Hill has focused investment in five sport "fields of play" including running, basketball, football (soccer), training, and sportswear. In January 2026, Nike introduced the Mind sneaker line, a series created on the basis of over 10 years of neuroscience-inspired materials science, and the Project Amplify bionic-powered performance platform.

Marketing spend by Nike, estimated to be around $5 billion in FY2026, is being used on branding, online platforms, and marketing activations. It directs the stories of athletes, cultural happenings, and the explosion of massive sporting events. Brand presence and commercial significance in sport-induced cultural events are extensive and culturally significant at Nike. In North America, Nike Digital also held zero promotional days in January and February 2026, compared to over 30 promotional days in January and February 2024.

The Path Back to $50 Billion: FY2027 Recovery Trajectory

Based on the agreement estimates, the turnover of Nike dropped to $46.3 billion in FY2025 as opposed to $51.4 billion in FY2024. The channel reset and inventory liquidation strain still took its toll on the top line, causing a slight decline to around $44.9 billion in FY2026. This is called the trough. The Nike revenue for FY2027 is characterized by a significant growth in categories and the recuperation of the expenditure in wholesale. Based on the Wall Street consensus of Seeking Alpha as of March 2026, the average analyst price target of NKE in 12 months is around $77.50. This is supported by the anticipation of a rapid pace of new product launches and an estimated recovery of revenues of about 8% in FY2027 as the channel inventories become balanced and the momentum in the wholesale gains momentum. Since revenue is likely to be near $50 billion within the near future and profits will resume to fall within the range of the bull-case ($85–$100), the most pertinent question is not whether a recovery will occur but rather when the EPS will recover enough to propel the margin growth beyond the current noise due to tariffs.

According to WallStreetZen's forecast across 41 analysts, the FY2026 EPS is projected to be approximately $1.59, which is lower than the $3.73 reported in FY2024 and higher than the earlier forecast. The majority of the costs were on tariff and restructuring, yet the growth in FY2027 was a huge 53% year-on-year. Consensus EPS values in FY2028 are more aligned at $3.11, which means that Nike still has a lot to regain in its earnings on a mid-cycle normalized basis. At the current price of approximately $53, NKE trades at roughly 22x FY2027E consensus EPS of $2.43—a meaningful discount to its historical mid-cycle multiple, reflecting the market’s continued caution ahead of the Q3 FY2026 earnings release. It is well positioned due to the quality of the brands under the holding franchise and the possibility of recovering EPS in FY2028. Analysts at TickerNerd report that the median price per share target in March 2026 is $84, which is an increase of 31% compared to the market. That is alright, but the bull case target of $120 (Oppenheimer/Brian Nagel) implies 87% upside from current levels.

Competitive Positioning: The Moat Competitors Cannot Cross

The best point against Nike in 2026 is that On Running and Hoka have stolen the market share of the brand, and this will never be recovered. It is a fine argument, but it fails to take into account the structural threat it presents to Nike and underestimates its competitive advantage. On Running and Hoka are two successful niche brands that have leveraged the consumer trend of maximalist-cushioning and Swiss-heritage aesthetics to sell products. Amazingly, they have grown in a real and impressive manner. The trailing twelve months revenue of On Running is approximately $2.4 billion, and it is under 5% of the current Nike annual run-rate revenue. Hoka, which is under Deckers Outdoor ownership, is similar in size and earns about $1.8 billion in revenue per year. The category leaders in the running market are not a major challenge to the dominance of a world brand with top standings in basketball, football, and training. On and Hoka are not concerned about sportswear.

The partnership between Nike and their athletes is too valuable to forgo. The Jordan Brand, a semi-autonomous unit of Nike, generates Nike about $6–$7 billion in yearly income—more income annually than On Running and Hoka together. The LeBron James, Cristiano Ronaldo, and Serena Williams franchises are powerful long-term assets that have helped create brand equity in various product lines. Nike is investing heavily in the FIFA World Cup 2026 (US and Canada), NBA Finals, and Champions League 2026. Few, if any, of the rival sporting brands have been able to utilize global sporting events on a scale with equal commercial success.

Share Price Context: Maximum Pessimism, Asymmetric Upside

The share price of NKE is an indicator of how the investors feel about the company, rather than the real business activities. The stock fell from approximately $73 in March 2025 to a 52-week low of $52.28, then recovered to approximately $68 following the Q2 FY2026 earnings beat, only to retrace further to the current $53 area as the market focused on forward margin guidance and the tariff environment. This is aligned with a stock that is at an early phase of recovery, and the indication of recovery is still present, but the link is not visible at once, and hence the highest negative correlation between price and intrinsic value. The current market capitalization, expressed as a valuation of approximately $82 billion, undervalues Nike by a large fraction of the long-run cash flow value, particularly given that the liquid assets on the balance sheet reflect a value of $8.35 billion and capital expenditure needs of less than sub-$5 billion annually.

The insider buying signal is particularly meaningful in this context. Elliott Hill, the CEO, had used $1 million dollars to buy NKE shares at prices in the $60s range. Another sign of commercially astute leadership in the company was the decision of its corporate executive chairman, Tim Cook to acquire NKE shares in late 2025. The long-term investors include Coldstream Capital Management, which has raised its interest in NKE by a half, and Exchange Traded Concepts LLC, which has raised its share by nearly 700%, according to the SEC Form 13F filings made in Q3 2025. An increase of 33% in the ownership of Soros Fund Management. The past has been determined to have a correlation between institutional purchases at the current levels and future returns above average.

Risk Factors That Merit Consideration

Greater China Structural Weakness

The Greater China business, historically, generating an estimated 15% of the annual revenue of the company, fell by 16% every year in the sixth consecutive quarter of Q2 FY2026. The reason why the Chinese domestic athletic footwear and apparel market has been going through a boom is because of the patriotic consumerism, lower price, and sponsoring sportsmen. The revelation of CEO Hill is that Greater China is grappling with structural difficulties in the market place, which will take time to overcome, and the FY2026 outlook of the third quarter promises to lead to an ongoing drag. Hill has called China a "big opportunity" in the long term and the near-term direction is uncertain and requires monitoring.

Tariff Headwind Magnitude

The incremental product cost of about $1.5 billion per annum in U.S. tariffs, about 315 basis points of gross margin headwind prior to mitigation, is a material cost that will continue to agitate throughout fiscal 2026. The suggested rebates in the supply chain to non-tariffed manufacturing facilities and price increments are likely to lower the net effect to just around 120 basis points, although supply chain transition is a very complicated process that may lead to delays. An increase in U.S. trade policy beyond current tariff levels would be an added strain to the recovery timeline.

Converse Brand Deterioration

Nike's Converse sub-brand --- historically a high-margin, culturally relevant sneaker franchise generating approximately $2 billion in annual revenue --- declined 30% in Q2 FY2026, representing a collapse that is more severe than channel cleaning or promotional repression. The Chuck Taylor All Star shape has remained the same over decades, and the Converse challenge poses a big threat to the traditional customers of the brand, consisting of young consumers. More fashion-conscious urban consumers have begun to move towards New Balance and other brands that are more culturally oriented. The lack of a clear public strategy is an issue with Converse.

Execution Risk During Multi-Year Reset

Meanwhile, Nike is forced to deal with the liquidation of its warehouse, the reconstruction of its wholesale channel, the reorganization of its supply chain, the development of a product innovation program, and the reorganization of its 8,000-strong organization. Implementing these measures may be difficult because the implementation of these actions may be resource constrained due to management bandwidth or other unforeseen external factors that postpone recovery when done simultaneously. The announcement of a restructuring fee of $300 million and the organizational realignments take care of the expense and the disturbance of repositioning a company like Nike at the pace that Hill saw it.

Bull and Bear Cases

The tariff environment stabilizes or even turns around, and Greater China becomes more stable in the second half of fiscal 2026. In accordance with such projections, the FY2027 earnings would grow from $2.43 to about $3.50 in FY2028 when the gross margins would revive to the range of 43--44%. A 24x forward multiple of FY2028E EPS would imply a share price of between $84--$90 by the end of 2027, with a margin recovery rate higher than expected provided it is above $100+. The next key landmark is the March 2026 Q3 earnings announcement: if gross margins improve sequentially or if China provides a favorable commentary, a significant re-rating is expected.

In the bear scenario, it is not possible to stabilize China, and then no meaningful steps can be made to bring about a reduction in the tariff burden and retardation of wholesale growth will be experienced when the channel-restock enthusiasm declines. The increase in the level of revenues at the trough in FY2026, the fall in the EPS below the consensus in FY2027, and the trough valuation of 20x decreasing earnings—between $48 and $52—show that investors are terrified by the structural defect of On and Hoka among others. Such a bear situation suggests that local Chinese brands have a more sustainable competitive challenge than the management considers at the current time. Bear conditions mean that there must be several parallel failures in the turnaround, and mean a level of brand decay that cannot yet be detected in the market share figures of Nike in the critical categories world-wide.

Conclusion: The Case for Strong Buy

Nike is not broken. The Air Jordan team has invented the science of performance footwear, and invented the most recognizable sports brand of all time, which has earned it up to $51 billion in revenue as recently as FY2024. The macroeconomic environment and three years of miscalculation strategies were extreme, and yet revenue was greater than expected in FY2024. In the short-term, there is a decline in investor confidence, and this will result in the highest level of worst-case pessimism and a long-term, fundamental investor. Such a point of entry is available today, with a price of about $53 per share.

The 20%+ running category growth sustained over two quarters is indicative of genuine inertia of the products rather than a promotional sham. The 8% growth in wholesale revenues, which follows the previously determined channel de-escalation, may be regarded as the structural reorganization of the Nike retailing partnerships. Accumulation of insiders, growth in institutional ownership, and the size of Q2 earnings beat are supporting the recovery thesis.

My $85 price target is derived as follows: I apply a 24x P/E multiple to the FY2027E EPS consensus of $2.43 (per Seeking Alpha). The 24x multiple is lower than the historical mid-cycle Nike P/E of 28–32x which reflects the execution and tariff risk that remains. The forecasts show that the 24x forward P/E rate of Nike multi-decade compounded history, which has risen to FY2027E, has not slowed down. Normalized tariffs and improved gross margins (top-line growth of 53% YoY) increased the EPS figure of the company to $2.43. Possibility of multiple expansion provided that milestones of recovery are achieved; one-year horizon with no assumed buyback acceleration. The figure of $85 is an increase of approximately 60% of its present level of $53. Dividend yield stands at 2.6%, giving the topside return target of more than 63%. Strong Buy.
